text_content:
We live in large subdivision located in fair size town in the Idaho mountains. We’ve had mountain lions found bedding under blue spruce trees in front yards in the middle of winter .  One took two cats in one night which could be extremely dangerous for young children and small adults who walk past within four feet of that same tree .   I’ve mentioned this to the neighbors and suggested that the branches at ground level should be removed to at least five feet above the ground but nothing has happened so I’m going to the city .
We’ve had similar situations in nearby subdivisions but instead of cats they were going after full grown elk , dragging them underneath a tree ten feet from my friends driveway and feeding on the elk for most of the winter and because of the deep snow we’re completely unseen .Next spring he trimmed the branches up to eight feet high. They have similar videos of a lion coming up to the picture windows eyeing their two dogs .

text_content: I'm puzzled why people in the comments are criticizing the security guard, when in fact, he was trying to save numerous lives. The tragic incident of the escaped lion highlights the importance of responsible animal ownership. While it's tempting to blame the security guard for shooting the lion, I firmly believe he made the right call to protect human lives. The real culprit, in my opinion, is the lion's owner, who kept a wild animal in captivity. By doing so, he not only put his own life at risk but also the lives of those around him. The lion's eventual escape and tragic demise were inevitable consequences of its unnatural confinement. The security guard's actions, though unfortunate, were necessary to prevent further harm to the community. Ultimately, this incident serves as a reminder of the dangers of keeping wild animals in domestic environments.

text_content: I'm a psychologist. I work with animals all the time. This one is easy. Big cats roar, small cats purr. Big cats have an innate "fight or flight" response to humans. Small cats do not. Cheetahs are the largest of the small cats. They may be apprehensive or frightened of you, but they won't attack you unless you threaten them. There has never been a recorded case of a cheetah attacking a human without being provoked first. And, to say they are "perfect pets" is the most ignorant thing you can say. Can they be domesticated? Yes. Should they be? No.

text_content:
I worked at a petting zoo that had some lemurs. They were *mean*. I was bit the one and only time I went into an enclosure to clean it. It was a small nip, basically equivalent to a cat scratch, but it wasn't casually brushed off, as I stopped working for the day and got it bandaged up.

I wasn't allowed to get close to the area where they had 3 aggressive lemurs (I cleaned an enclosure with only one, much calmer overall lemur), and all the enclosures had basically two doors on them, with a little area in between, and you always kept at least one door closed at all times.

They also had much more going on in their areas, with toys and whatnot, and they were routinely rotated throughout 3 different enclosures so they had more variety in their life. All this was for lemurs, who are both much smaller add far less dangerous than chimpanzees, but were very clearly treated much better and held to a higher safety standard by this small business rentable petting zoo than anyone in this video working with large, dangerous, understimulated great apes.

text_content:
The story Joe is referring to is St. James Davis & his wife LaDonna Davis and their chimpanzee Moe.

After Moe had bit several people in West Covina, California, the chimp was relocated to an animal sanctuary. 

When the two came to visit, they brought birthday cake, toys, chocolate, and even candy hearts. They were seated at a picnic table celebrating his birthday when two adult male chimps, Buddy & Ollie rushed the wife and bit her thumb off. St. James pushed his wife underneath the table to protect her and the chimps began attacking him simultaneously. They attacked his face including his nose and lips, his left foot, destroyed most of his fingers, ripped off his testicles, his buttocks, part of his torso. The sanctuary’s owners son-in-law retrieved a .45 pistol and shot the chimp Buddy in the head. Ollie dragged St. James body down an empty corridor where the son-in-law then killed him by gunshot. Their chimp Moe was not involved in the attack, but it’s unclear if he tried to save or protect either of them. 

St. James spent six months in the hospital recovering, some of which he was in a coma. He underwent 60 surgeries, has a glass eye, and two slits where his nose used to be.

Interestingly enough, in 2008 the Davises chimp Moe escaped from his sanctuary. There have been reports of sightings near homes where he was last housed, as well as claims he was heading toward a mountain. The Davises hired a helicopter to do flyovers of the San Bernardino National Forest in an attempt to find him… but to date the 42-year old chimp has never been found. 

Thank you Wikipedia.

video_description: Can Pygmy Marmosets be Pets? Do Finger Monkeys Make Good Pets? As one can imagine, it's easy to fall for their cute faces ...
text_content:
No.  Monkeys - in general - do NOT make "good" pets.

Unless you are very well-prepared and aware of what monkeys are and do, don't get a monkey. They are too intelligent, curious, and possessive to be a good room mate. They will shit and piss anywhere, and at any time.  You cannot physically discipline a monkey, nor is it easy to do so by restricting them from activity or out of cage freedoms. They will bear a grudge. They will bite if you attempt to lift it off the couch, and throw their shit at you as you pass the cage.

This is why they end up mean and vicious, and needing rescue.

DON'T get a monkey.

text_content:
Slow loris
"Their huge round eyes, adorable faces and almost human-like paws have made slow lorises one of the internet's favourite animals. Being nocturnal, the little primates seem almost docile during the day: when they do move around, they do so slowly and deliberately. When threatened, they tend to freeze.
But slow lorises also have a lethal ace up their sleeve, almost literally.
In extreme cases, bite recipients may enter anaphylactic shock, sometimes resulting in death
When threatened, a loris will often raise its arms above its head. Far from being a sign of surrender, this is a defensive posture, bringing a gland around its elbow close to its mouth.
By combining the oil secreted from the gland with saliva, the loris creates a strong venom, which it then smears onto the top of its head, making it a toxic morsel for any predator.
The loris can also keep the venom inside its mouth, delivering it in a bite. The incisors at the front of the bottom jaw help to conduct the liquid upwards, ensuring it gets into any wound it can inflict.
According to Anne-Isola Nekaris of Oxford Brookes University, a bite from a slow loris is "intensely painful". "In extreme cases, bite recipients may enter anaphylactic shock, sometimes resulting in death," Nekaris says.
There are nine known species of slow loris living in the forests of South East Asia. The venom of the pygmy slow loris (Nycticebus pygmaeus) has been found to contain 220 compounds.
So, the next time you watch a video of a slow loris being "tickled" with its arms in the air, remember it is not enjoying the experience: it is waiting for a chance to strike."

video_title: Do SQUIRREL MONKEYS make good PETS?
video_description: Who hasn't dreamt about having a pet monkey? In this video, we look into what it actually takes to have a squirrel monkey as a pet ...
text_content: Knew a family who had a tiny squirrel monkey for almost 30 years.  He slept in his cage but knew how to open the door so essentially ran free in their house.   He was housetrained to use his cage which was lined with newspaper but preferred the cats' litterboxes (they also had up to 3 cats).  The cats initially tried stalking him because he was tiny but he quickly demonstrated that he could put some serious hurt on them if he wanted to.   He could be destructive, and I wouldn't recommend one as a pet to anyone else.  But they had a large family with a lot of kids around to play with him and keep an eye on him.  They had to keep their cupboards locked because he knew how to open them and would tear open cereal boxes and smash peanut butter and jelly jars on the floor if given the opportunity.  He had astonishing speed, leaping ability, and coordination and so 'won' almost any game of tag or keep-away he played with the kids.  He escaped the house a few times and was un-catchable.  During his escapes, he lived in the trees and ate insects and raided the neighbors' bird feeders and any pet food they left outside, but would be back tapping at a window on the first cold night.

text_content: I worked at a doggy daycare and my boss had a couple cockatoos.  She was the perfect owner for one tho, well knowledged about all animals, single and lonely as mean as that might sound, and lived in a pet shop so she had the perfect place to store the parrots.    You can’t put into perspective how intimidating they are tho, sure they might act cute and talk but anytime that beak is near you there is a chance you’ll get bit.    I used to have to sweep the floor and the cockatoos would follow me around the whole time If they were out their cage trying to bite my legs.    If they were in the cage anytime I tried to sweep under a cage I had a massive beak sticking between the bars trying to bite me or the broom.     Not to mention the when they are out of the cage it is the loudest screeching you’ll ever hear, just constant parrot squawking at a 10/10 volume level for as long as they want to squawk which can be hours.    I love cockatoos to death but I’d never consider owning one but any chance I ever get to interact with one I’ll take the opportunity.

text_content: Remember, they’re $3,000 unpredictable, and having one isn’t just a commitment, it’s a lifestyle. Relationships, plans, vacations, and more all affect you, and your bird. They also have sharp beaks, and can easily tear you open if provoked or scared. As I said unpredictable, they also have their mood swings. And don’t forget breeding season. They can become very territorial and hormonal. They may attack, or get aroused by the things their owner does with them. It’s best just to not touch them until the season is over is this state. And as the guy said in the video, they can live a very, VERY long time. More than 40-60 years. You’re looking more at 80, or closer to 100. Most humans don’t live that long. Many people inherit them from their grandparents, or even put them in their wills. Serious inquires only please. And if you must have a cockatoo, get either a Goffin, or a cockaTIEL instead. My cockatiel is great and very friendly. He whistles to us, and is affectionate. They’re much cheaper, and live closer to 20 or 25 years so much less of a commitment and less time consuming. But it still takes effort. It takes a very special person to own these birds, and I hope you think carefully, and responsibly about this. Thank you so much.

text_content: I've had a cockatoo and I've had a blue front Amazon.  I would never let them be close to each other. Cockatoos are extremely intelligent birds. They have the emotional intelligence of a 5-year-old. Their  jealousy drives them to harm or kill their rival which could be a guinea pig or a hamster, or a cat or a dog.  They will go after them if they think that you're not giving them enough attention and love. I ended up giving our cockatoo to a vet lab technician who had always wanted a cockatoo and she had no other animals so it's a perfect home for him.  So this cockatoo is not showing dominance only, he is showing that he will attack and he will harm that bird.  The cockatoo I had, named Kazoo,  had to be pulled off of my African Grey, named Pepper, and he had punctured her cheek and her wing.  And that was not the first incident I had with a cockatoo he went after my darling little Harley bird who is what we call a pocket parrot.  He hurt Harley so badly in his wing we had to amputate the wing as it was destroyed. Harley was a feisty little parrot and he climbed off of his cage one day and marched over to Kazoos cage because he was going to finish what Kazoo had started and I'm so glad I was there to scoop him up and lock him back in his cage or he would have been killed that day.  Something I find very appalling on the internet with animals is people get animals they don't understand they don't know anything about their breed or what the needs are for that individual animal.  I handled Birds since I was 10 years old and I'm in my 70s now and I am appalled at how little people understand are know about birds they are highly intelligent more so than a dog is believe it or not.

text_content: My parents bought a macaw before they had children. Macaws & children DON'T mix. My parents had the macaw locked up in a cage for 17 years because macaws can't handle children so she would bite the us. Macaws can bite through broomsticks so you can imagine how bad the bites were. When I turned 12 I "saved her" & developed a relationship with her. We were insperatable until she passed away 2 years ago. So I took care of her for 12 years. So PLEASE do NOT get a macaw if you plan on having children. While in the cage she plucked out the majority of her feathers out of stress of being locked up 24/7. The feathers never grew back & she developed PTSD. Please don't get a macaw if you want children

text_content: I work behavioral issue macaws to help them become adoption candidates. This is very true. Most bites I’ve gotten have come from fear. I’ve worked with macaws who have been through insane amounts of abuse, I’ve worked with birds that would bite everyone and everything, yet they never hurt me to the point of needing stitches or permanent disfigurement. I’ve gotten some nasty bites here and there, I’ve collected a few scars even, but it’s a far cry from the real damage these guys can do. They are pretty gentle by nature. I love seeing how they transform and learn better boundaries as well. I learn  how to keep that bird from feeling the need to bite. Birds will bite but most of the time they give very clear warnings before hand. It definitely is about knowing your bird as well, birds can bite not just because they’re scared but also because they are over stimulated. Be gentle, patient, and kind with a biting bird. Most will learn boundaries over time or at least you will learn how to pick up on the warning signs.

video_title: The Alligator Snapping Turtle is a MENACE
text_content: When I was a kid, my buddy lived on a farm with some pretty big ponds on the property. The biggest pond had swans and all of a sudden they started losing feet. Turns out there was a 4ft wide, 5ft long alligator snapper in the pond. We used to swim and fish in that pond all the time. When my buddy's mom finally got a picture of the snapper up on the bank, I got chills knowing I was swimming with that thing. Noone ever swam in that pond again lol. Nope, nope, and nope. I've moved smaller ones out of the road here and there and when they snap it sounds like a trap snapping shut. Your finger would be like a toothpick. Also be careful because these sobs have long necks. Just when you think you're out of bite range, you're not. They're amazing animals that have survived forever. Looking at one up close is like looking back in time, like a living dinosaur.

text_content: I have moved multiple box turtles off the road in the direction they were facing- or if that direction looks dangerous, into nearby woods. Recently I stopped for a 6" male that had a 1" piece of shell broken off and hanging. The wound was dirty and festering a little. He was on a super steep dangerous part of Hwy 52 so I brought him home, cleaned and treated the wound, and glued the shell shard back in place with cyanoacrylate. That held nicely, and I am monitoring him to ensure infection doesnt grow. I made him a 6' outdoor pen with food, water, shade, shelter, and plants. He sure sleeps a lot, and I have not observed him eating, so maybe he is sad, or in pain, or both. Soon as the wound heals fully I will release him (back near where I found him if I can find a safe spot). Thanks for an excellent video!

text_content: Ok, out of kindness here is some turtle care needs and warnings for keeping turtles, I looked though the comments and noticed some people had outdated or incorrect turtle care recommendations and stuff. I just need people to understand this before getting turtles because I had read every comment and I do not want anyone getting turtles and unknowingly putting them at risk. And the one thing that bothered me the most in the comments is “you should get more turtles” or “Get him a friend “ like idk if you guys know but NO! 👉TURTLES👏 ARE 👏NOT 👏SOCIAL👏 ANIMALS 👏AND 👏THEY 👏WILL 👏HAVE 👏A 👏HIGH 👏POSSIBILITY 👏FOR👏 HARMING👏 IF 👏NOT 👏KILLING 👏EACH 👏OTHER! 👏IF 👏YOU 👏WANT 👏MORE👏 THEN 👏ONE 👏TURLE 👏IN👏THE👏SAME👏TANK👏THEN 👏YOU 👏WOULD 👏NEED 👏A 👏LARGER 👏TANK! 👏AND 👏EVEN 👏THEN 👏THERE 👏WOULD👏 ALWAYS 👏HAVE 👏A 👏RISK 👏OF 👏THEM👏BEING 👏HURT 👏OR 👏DYING! 👈 I’M NOT SAYING TO BE RUDE, I AM SAYING THAT TO PREVENT ANY IMPULSE BUYING THAT WOULD LEAD TO A HURT OR DEAD TURTLE AND PREVENTING A NEW OWNER FROM LEARNING THE HARD WAY THAT CAN BE HEARTBREAKING! 💔 So if you get a turtle the general rule for aquatic turtles is 10 gallons of water per inch of shell length, meaning a 4-inch turtle needs a minimum 40-gallon tank, and adults often require 75-120+ gallons; PUR TURTLE, DOUBLE THAT AMOUNT FOR TWO THOUGH I WOULD NOT RECOMMEND HOMEING THEM TOGETHER! this provides enough swimming space and accounts for their growth, but remember it's a minimum, and larger is always better. Always have a basking area that a turtle can get completely dry on and with that they need heat and UVB, preferably not the conjoined double lamp heat and UVB because the UVB light  in those kits aren’t the best, instead a tube UVB light that’s a 5T light is more recommend and make sure both the heat and UVB light hit the same basking area, also a water filter is also important and because turtles are messy, I say for example if you have one baby turtle in a 20 gallon long tank get a filter that is at least met for a 40-55 gallons (that what works for me at least with the tiny baby turtle) and as your turtle grows they will need to be upgraded so if you wanna save on money look into buying second hand tanks or using storage tubs or stock tanks, you don’t even have to fill the tank up all the way if it’s still a baby, you can start with a 75 gallon tank for example and keep it shallow and just raise the water level as it grows (keep in mind the water should be deep enough for it to swim but shallow enough to easily reach the surface to breathe, typically 1.5 to 2 times its shell length, or about 4-8 inches, gradually increasing as it grows stronger, ensuring it can always stand on its hind legs and touch the bottom or easily get to the surface.) and as for plants it depends on the turtle, I have some turtles that would ignore the plants (like my Mississippi map turtle) who would eat the romaine lettuce and sliced carrots but won’t eat the guppy grass in his tank or the hornwort. Or my baby red belly cooter who would eat every plant I give her besides the hornwort. Turns out turtles in the wild don’t care for hornwort do to the texture of the plant even if it’s safe to eat. None of my turtle eat or even like hornwort so you can try hornwort if you like just keep in mind it’s a floating plant,doesn’t need substrate. Also when it comes to substrate there is many options to choose from, you can do sand, tile, no substrate, large river rocks (make sure they are too big for the turtle to eat) but I would stay away from aquarium gravel because it can cause  impaction if eaten and there is many bad stores of turtles dying from aquarium gravel. As for decoration make sure that it’s something that would NOT crush the turtles, something that wouldn’t risk the turtle getting stuck, something that does not have blade like edges,and something that is aquarium safe that the turtle won’t eat, sometimes people use plastic plants but for me personally I wouldn’t trust it because I heard some turtles nip at it thinking it’s a real plant at first and we wouldn’t want our beloved turtles to eat plastic. Another thing is food, do NOT feed turtles  goldfish, rosy red minnows(any any minnows),and other cyprinids (like common minnows, dace) away from turtles because they contain thiaminase, an enzyme that destroys Vitamin B1, causing neurological damage, paralysis, and death in turtles, and also avoid aggressive fish like Cichlids that can harm the turtle's eyes. Slow, colorful, or long-finned fish like Bettas, Koi, or large Plecos are also bad choices as they are easy prey or attract unwanted attention, while fast, small livebearers (guppies, mollies, platies, and mosquito fish) are better options for enrichment or occasional treats, but always monitor for safety. Also turtles need a water heater, even more so in their baby and juvenile year! As for main food pellets there is many options just try and keep it diverse and try to avoid as much of the low quality ones if possible, for me personally I use Ultra Fresh Floating Turtle food, zoo med turtle food, and mazuri turtle food. Also almost forgot but make sure you had a cuddle bone for calcium,turtles need it for their shell health. ANYWAY Please make sure to do your research before getting a turtle let alone any pet, turtles are a big commitment and can live for many years! And if one day you end up not being able to care for the turtle do NOT release it into the wild, turtles brought up in captivity can not survive and if they do they can become invasive in some areas, best to responsibility find the turtle a new home if one day you decide you don’t want it or can’t care for it anymore!

text_content: Having had two a literal rattlesnakes in the past, I recommend going with the biggest a literal rattlesnake you can find. The big a literal rattlesnakes are full of bluster, but rarely a problem. However, the small a literal rattlesnakes are much more bite than bark. I had a pretty big Mojave Green that, while outwardly aggressive, never made an actual bite attempt. The little sidewinder I don’t think I heard rattle more than a few times, but she would strike at the drop of a hat and was mind-bogglingly fast. I took them both seriously, but I always kept extra on guard any time I had to handle the little’un.

text_content:
I have a true story about a snake. I'm italian so this will be hard for me to tell in english, but I'll try. 
My beloved grandfather fought in ww2, but he was captured and he sadly had to spent seven years of his life as a prisoner of war in South Africa. They were in a camp and a certain day they woke up and they were told that they had to move. He had an old folded piece of fabric as a pillow and when he lifted it up he found out there was a viper under it. He had probably spent the night with this lethal snake under his head. Everybody wanted to kill the animal, but my grandaddy told them that they should let him live and go, just like the snake did with him... I was a little girl but I remember how proud of him I was... still miss him every day.

text_content: I'd rather separate a hatched clutch of burms or retics than a clutch of corn snakes lol. Burms and retics are actually more mindful of when and where they're biting so you can be more careful. Not that bites really hurt as hatchlings from either species  but fucking corns just mindlessly bite lmao. I've had three biting me on one finger and one of the ones biting me was getting gnawed on by it's sibiling. Plus opening up a box of baby burms or retics hissing is actually impressive cuz for being so small it's still loud lol. Some corns make almost like puffing noises when they're hissing, some sound like they're hyperventilating, and other sound like they're running out of oxygen and going to die XD. Don't me me wrong I love all forms of life and respect all forms, I even have an anery, butter stripe and a reverse oketee corn. All I'm saying for the most part a lot of baby snakes can and will have simmilar attitudes even if they're different species.none of them have a relationship or even experience with a human so. Reptiles are really fun and rewarding but if you're scared of being bitten then it's probably not for you. That's not to say that every or potentially even any reptile will even bite you but it's possible. Snakes mainly you should just plan to be bitten. That's not meaning  by every snake you ever handle I mean that as in if you've had the chance to handle more than probably 7-8 snakes you've probably been bit or at least struck at.

text_content: There was an Argus monitor on a construction site I worked on. There was a trench for gas pipes that was open awaiting the pipe, each day this massive Argus would drop into the trench and do his shopping, eating every other animal in there from giant burrowing cockroaches to  skinks, elapid snakes and white's tree frogs. The tracks in the sand at the bottom of the trench showed every pursuit and capture. I eventually managed to catch that monitor one day, hauled him out of the trench and was attacked several times when I released him in the bush. By far the craziest monitor I have handled.

text_content:
For those confused.

Venomous animals inject their toxins, typically through bites or stings, while poisonous animals secrete or excrete toxins that are harmful when touched or ingested.
The most venomous is a Box Jellyfish

Edit for those saying the cone snail

Box jellyfish venom causes instant, severe pain and can lead to cardiac arrest and death within minutes, while a cone snail's venom causes paralysis over hours, potentially leading to respiratory and heart failure, but death is rarer and takes longer. The box jellyfish is considered the most venomous marine animal and is a direct threat to human life, whereas the geography cone snail's venom is also highly dangerous, but death is nearly exclusive to one species and is less common than from box jellyfish stings. The National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) considers The Box Jellyfish to be the most venomous marine animal in the world.

Remember, you gotta feed it and look after it. And there's the risk it can attack others (huge lawsuit/prison) or attack you instead (disabled/dead).

2- Hermit Crab (even cheaper and easier to own)
3- Ostrich (okay, you will need a farm. Kinda dangerous but manageable)
4- Tiger (no way. You will basically become a zoo keeper)
5- Gorilla (cheaper and less dangerous than a Tiger, but note in many places Gorilla's have rights similar to Humans. So their care and habitat is to a much higher standard, meaning you have to be extremely wealthy to even consider this. Much more so than a Tiger).

video_title: How Raccoons (Almost) Got Away With It
video_description: Nessie's Linktree- https://linktr.ee/Lochnessofficial TikTok: https://www.tiktok.com/@lochnessofficial?_r=1&_t=ZT-93hL3Ntn1mY ...
text_content:
EDITED TO ADD
I wasn’t expecting so many reactions, so could you do me a favor and read through the thread before asking questions or making comments to see if yours has already been answered? I’m repeating myself a lot. 

Exotic mammals veterinarian here. I treated pet and wild raccoons for three decades and I’m here to tell you the babies are super-cute and friendly. BUT…once they reach adulthood, most of them just want you dead. I saw more owners get bitten by raccoons than any other pet. You have to sedate them to do literally anything to them, even brush them.

They’re smart, they’re cute, they’re funny—all those things—as long as they get to do anything they want. If you try to stop them from eating your kitchen cabinets or shredding your mattress, it’s over. Not to mention that yes, they wash their food in bowls. But, what most people don’t know is that they also poop in those bowls. 

They make terrible pets. Enjoy them in the wild and leave them alone.

I’ll also add this: 

Raccoons are one of the Big Four rabies vector species. In most of Europe, they’re considered such a dangerous invasive species that they can be hunted, trapped, or shot on sight. In most U.S. states, you need a special permit to own one. 

They also have a high incidence of what’s known as the “raccoon roundworm,” which can be transmitted to dogs and people. In people, it invades the brain and can cause all kinds of neurological symptoms, including death. Children are at most risk, because transmission is from fecal contamination of soil, i.e., putting dirty fingers in mouths. 

Fortunately, human deaths from raccoon rabies or roundworm are rare, but please don’t push it by trying to make one a pet.
